_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Warren County, NJ. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Warren was created from Sussex County in 1824. Records begin at that date; earlier records in Sussex County at Newton.
display_name: Warren County, New Jersey — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: Belvidere, NJ — Mansfield Street (with modern additions)

previous_courthouses.location: Belvidere — temporary quarters after separation from Sussex County

record_losses.cause: County creation — Warren carved from Sussex in 1824
record_losses.scope: structural — pre-1824 records in Sussex County at Newton
record_losses.description: Warren County's independent records begin in 1824. Pre-1824 records for the area are in Sussex County at Newton. No major fire or disaster has destroyed Warren County courthouse records since formation.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in what became Warren County before 1824, the records are in Sussex County at Newton — the parent county.

deeds.completeness: Complete from formation. Pre-1824 in Sussex County.
deeds.repository: Warren County Clerk's Office, Belvidere; NJ State Archives

wills_estates.repository: Warren County Surrogate's Office; NJ State Archives

marriages.completeness: Good from formation (NJ licenses from 1795; Warren created 1824).

court_minutes.repository: Warren County Clerk's Office; NJ State Archives

vital_records.completeness: Spotty until 1900. Rural compliance was lower than urban areas.
vital_records.repository: NJ State Archives; NJ Department of Health

substitute_sources.description: All civil records for what became Warren County before 1824 are in Sussex County files at Newton. Deeds, wills, court minutes must be searched there for pre-1824 families.
substitute_sources.repository: Sussex County Clerk's Office, Newton; NJ State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for any Warren County family with roots before 1824.
substitute_sources.source: Snell, History of Sussex and Warren Counties (1881).

substitute_sources.source_type: German and Dutch Reformed church records
substitute_sources.description: The Kittatinny Valley was settled by German and Dutch families whose church records (Lutheran, Reformed) predate civil vital records. The German Reformed church at Greenwich and the Dutch Reformed churches in the area hold records from the colonial era.
substitute_sources.repository: Reformed Church in America Archives; NJ State Archives; Warren County Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for German and Dutch families. Church records document families for generations before civil vital registration.
substitute_sources.source: Snell, History of Sussex and Warren Counties (1881).

substitute_sources.description: The Morris Canal terminated at Phillipsburg in Warren County. Canal company records include employee records, boat registrations, and inclined-plane operation logs.
substitute_sources.repository: NJ State Archives; Canal Society of New Jersey; Warren County Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Useful for families in canal-related employment — boat operators, lock keepers, plane tenders, and laborers.

narrative_hooks: Warren County records begin in 1824 — before that, look in Sussex County at Newton. If {ancestor_name} farmed the Kittatinny Valley before 1824, the deed is a county away.

German and Dutch Reformed church records are essential for Warren County's earliest families — if {ancestor_name} was German, the church register at Greenwich or the Dutch church holds records the courthouse cannot.

The Morris Canal records at Phillipsburg document the transportation workers — if {ancestor_name} operated a canal boat or tended an inclined plane, the canal company records are at the NJ State Archives.

Warren County's rural courthouse at Belvidere has served continuously since 1827 — if {ancestor_name}'s deed or will was recorded there, the document survives in the same building where it was originally filed.
source_references: Warren County Clerk's Office. Guide to Warren County Records.
Snell, James P. History of Sussex and Warren Counties. Philadelphia: Everts and Peck, 1881.
Lee, James. The Morris Canal. York, PA: Canal Captain's Press, 1979.
